answer:
**The title "Saqaliya" (صقلية), the Arabic name for Sicily, directly addresses the island itself—personified throughout the poem as a grieving entity bearing the ruins of lost Islamic ("Hejazi") civilization.**[^1][^2]

This apostrophe appears explicitly midway through: "Ah, O Sicily! You are the honor of the sea, / A guide upon this desert made of water."[^3] The opening lines set the elegiac tone by urging an "eye that rains down tears of blood" to weep over the island's grave-like state.[^1] Iqbal positions himself as a sorrow-bearer, inheriting its mourning ("To sorrow-fated Iqbal, your mourning was bequeathed") and vowing to carry its story "as a gift to Hindustan" to stir Indian Muslims.[^4]

A title like "Sicily" (the Italian/English name) would have failed to invoke the island's **Arabic-Islamic heritage** for Iqbal's primary Urdu-speaking Muslim readership. "Saqaliya" resonates with the *adab* (belles-lettres) tradition, echoing medieval Arab geographers and poets who celebrated Muslim Sicily (827–1091 CE) as a pinnacle of civilization—contrasting its current desolation under Western rule. This choice frames the poem as a *ruth* (pathos-laden lament) within Islamic memory, linking Sicily's "tumult of those desert-dwellers" (Arab conquerors) to broader losses like Baghdad, Delhi, and Granada.[^2][^5]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 21 "Saqaliya" ([p-08760e](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-08760e)).
[^2]: Ibid., [p-13e58a](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-13e58a).
[^3]: Ibid., [p-379aeb](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-379aeb).
[^4]: Ibid., [p-efa635](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-efa635)) and [p-665fb2](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/#p-665fb2).
[^5]: Ibid., full chapter (/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/21-saqaliya/translation/).
